summary: After her son broke his leg in a soccer accident, O'Neil experienced the kindness of New York City cab drivers who showed extraordinary patience and care while helping transport her injured child. She contrasts the stereotype of an anonymous, pitiless city with the reality of these workers who went far beyond their job requirements. One driver in particular managed to move her son so smoothly he felt like he was levitating. The post is a heartfelt tribute to the everyday humanity of service workers.
key_points: Cab drivers showed remarkable patience despite the economic cost of waiting for a crying, injured child
One cabbie's strength and gentleness made the painful transfer feel almost effortless
These acts of kindness aren't exceptional but represent what O'Neil believes is normal behavior for cabbies
In a city that seems anonymous, service workers provide crucial human connection
money_quotes: He was 'infinitely strong and infinitely patient' and somehow managed to port my son backwards across the back seat in a perfect, smooth motion, that made him think he was levitating.
I don't even think I was particularly lucky; I think that's actually pretty normal for cabbies.
In a big, crowded city that might seem anonymous and pitiless, it's a super comforting feeling to know they are there.
They are some really great people, willing and able to help out strangers all the time.
It's not good money to be waiting around for a crying 7-year-old to calm down and move one more inch.
key_insight: The everyday kindness of service workers like cab drivers provides an essential human safety net in cities that might otherwise feel cold and anonymous.
